Title: Sphingolipid homeostasis: How do cells know when enough is enough? Implications for plant pathogen responses

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to sphingolipid catabolism and modulation of PCD responses
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes LCB catabolism; dpl1 mutants show enhanced FB1-induced programmed cell death without obvious phenotype under optimal growth
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DPL1 encodes LCB-1-phosphate lyase in the catabolic branch of sphingolipid metabolism. 2. Null mutants do not markedly alter growth in optimal conditions. 3. Loss enhances FB1-induced PCD, implicating catabolism in buffering sphingolipid stress.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced buffering of sphingolipid stress can enhance cell death
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a catabolic homeostasis gene discussed in the review
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for modulating stress-induced PCD thresholds, but likely unsuitable for broad enhancement due to cell death risk

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts ceramides to ceramide-1-phosphate and suppresses ceramide-induced PCD; acd5 mutants accumulate free ceramides and show early senescence
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACD5 encodes ceramide kinase that attenuates ceramide bioactivity. 2. Mutation causes ceramide accumulation and early senescence. 3. The phenotype is stronger when combined with alkaline ceramidase defect, indicating catabolic cooperation in ceramide homeostasis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; genetic interaction with acer
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss causes early senescence and excess free ceramides
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Represents a non-enzymatic-branch control on bioactive ceramide levels
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be used to tune ceramide signaling thresholds, but senescence risk is substantial

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Ceramide turnover contributes to limiting PCD-active ceramide pools
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes ceramide turnover; loss accentuates free ceramide accumulation and senescence when combined with acd5
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACER encodes alkaline ceramidase for ceramide turnover. 2. It acts with ceramide kinase-mediated control to limit ceramide accumulation. 3. Combined defects exacerbate senescence and ceramide overaccumulation.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced ceramide turnover increases senescence risk
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed in catabolic balancing section of the review
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential modifier of ceramide turnover, but likely useful only in tightly controlled engineering contexts

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major membrane structural components; excessive intermediates are associated with programmed cell death and pathogen responses
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Primary regulatory point; first committed step
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses serine and palmitoyl-CoA to form 3-ketosphinganine
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for de novo sphingolipid synthesis and growth; altered ORM interaction deregulates long-chain base biosynthesis and ceramide accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LCB1 is a core SPT subunit forming the active site with LCB2. 2. Loss of LCB1 function causes pollen lethality or severe growth defects, showing essentiality for sphingolipid production. 3. A mutant LCB1 lacking the transmembrane domain phenocopies ORM-defective deregulation, indicating the LCB1 transmembrane domain mediates ORM-dependent negative regulation of SPT.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant characterization; RNAi suppression; transgenic expression of TMD-deleted LCB1; cryo-EM structural analysis
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: LCB2a; LCB2b; ssSPTa; ssSPTb; ORM1; ORM2
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Complete loss causes pollen lethality; strong suppression causes severe dwarfing
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review paper summarizing Arabidopsis SPT complex organization and homeostatic control
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating LCB1-ORM interaction could tune sphingolipid homeostasis and defense, but strong perturbation is likely lethal or causes severe growth penalties

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major membrane structural components; excessive intermediates are associated with programmed cell death and pathogen responses
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Primary regulatory point; first committed step
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Works with LCB1 in the SPT active site to condense serine and palmitoyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential SPT subunit for sphingolipid biosynthesis and viability
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LCB2a is one of two Arabidopsis LCB2 subunits of SPT. 2. It contributes to the enzyme active site and pyridoxal phosphate-dependent catalysis. 3. Loss of LCB2 function is lethal or severely growth impairing, underscoring its essential role in sphingolipid precursor biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of SPT function causes lethality or severe dwarfing
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Cryo-EM structure highlighted LCB2 N terminus interaction with ORM N terminus in inhibitory conformation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Limited direct engineering utility because severe perturbation compromises viability

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major membrane structural components; excessive intermediates are associated with programmed cell death and pathogen responses
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Primary regulatory point; first committed step
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Works with LCB1 in the SPT active site to condense serine and palmitoyl-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential SPT catalytic subunit involved in ceramide-responsive repression by ORM
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LCB2b is a catalytic SPT subunit carrying the PLP-linked catalytic lysine. 2. Cryo-EM revealed ceramide positioned between LCB2b and ORM1. 3. The LCB2 N terminus participates in the inhibitory interface formed when ceramide-bound ORM blocks palmitoyl-CoA entry.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cryo-EM structural analysis; in vitro activity assays
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong loss of function compromises viability
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: K311 in LCB2b is the PLP-linked residue noted in the review
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for fine-tuning sphingolipid flux, but direct perturbation likely risky for growth

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Accessory subunit of first committed step
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Palmitoyl-CoA preference determinant within SPT complex
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Noncatalytic SPT subunit that stimulates SPT activity and influences acyl-CoA substrate chain-length preference
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for normal SPT activity and viability; helps enforce palmitoyl-CoA specificity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ssSPTa is an SPT small subunit required for full activity of the enzyme complex. 2. Complete loss causes pollen lethality, showing essentiality. 3. Its Met19 residue gates the substrate tunnel and influences acyl-CoA chain-length preference.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Arabidopsis ssSPTa and ssSPTb both strongly prefer palmitoyl-CoA; M19V expands substrate usage
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible route to alter substrate selectivity of sphingolipid biosynthesis, but viability constraints are substantial

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Accessory subunit of first committed step
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Palmitoyl-CoA preference determinant within SPT complex
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Noncatalytic SPT small subunit that stimulates activity and helps determine acyl-CoA substrate specificity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to SPT function and substrate preference in sphingolipid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ssSPTb is a small SPT subunit homolog in Arabidopsis. 2. SPT complexes containing Arabidopsis ssSPT subunits strongly prefer palmitoyl-CoA. 3. Residues equivalent to Met19 help determine chain-length preference.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review emphasizes ssSPT subunit effects on substrate specificity rather than direct nutrient trait engineering
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential for engineering altered sphingoid base precursor profiles via substrate-specificity tuning

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: LAG1 homolog; Class II ceramide synthase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Class II ceramide synthesis; trihydroxy LCB-VLCFA ceramide branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Trihydroxy LCB-VLCFA ceramides / GIPC-enriched ceramide pool
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports basal membrane sphingolipid functions and contributes to homeostatic repression of SPT
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream branch point toward Class II ceramides
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-defining enzyme, not explicitly rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Trihydroxy LCBs; very-long-chain acyl-CoAs, especially 24:0-CoA and 26:0-CoA
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: N-acylates trihydroxy long-chain bases with very-long-chain fatty acyl-CoAs to form ceramides
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms branch ceramide products that repress upstream SPT via ORM
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes production of phytoceramide-type ceramides that support growth and feedback repression of SPT
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LOH1 is a Class II ceramide synthase with strong specificity for trihydroxy LCBs and VLCFAs. 2. Its products are proposed to bind ORM and repress SPT activity for sphingolipid homeostasis. 3. LOH1 is more highly expressed than LOH3 and is highly sensitive to FB1 inhibition.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant/transgenic analysis; substrate specificity assays; FB1 sensitivity analysis; sphingolipidomics
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: FB1 sensitivity makes this branch vulnerable; altering LOH1 may affect growth-related GIPC homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Class II ceramide products are enriched in GIPCs and are associated with repression of de novo sphingolipid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be used to reinforce homeostatic restraint and membrane stability, but may dampen defense-associated sphingolipid bursts

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: LAG1 homolog; Class I ceramide synthase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Class I ceramide synthesis; dihydroxy LCB-C16 ceramide branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Dihydroxy LCB-C16 ceramides / GlcCer-enriched ceramide pool
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Associated with programmed cell death, immune responses, and override of ORM-mediated repression
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream branch point toward Class I ceramides
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-switch enzyme for homeostatic override
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: N-acylates dihydroxy long-chain bases with C16 fatty acyl-CoAs to form ceramides
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms branch ceramide products that fail to repress upstream SPT and can maintain high pathway flux
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enhances accumulation of dihydroxy ceramides, elevated sphingolipid content, dwarfing, defense gene expression, and PCD-associated responses
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LOH2 is a Class I ceramide synthase specific for dihydroxy LCBs and C16 acyl-CoAs. 2. Increased flux through LOH2 leads to hyperaccumulation of dihydroxy ceramides, elevated total sphingolipids, dwarfing, and activation of defense responses. 3. LOH2-derived ceramides are proposed to override ORM-mediated SPT repression, enabling sphingolipid bursts during pathogen response or FB1 perturbation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression lines; mutant analysis; FB1 treatment; sphingolipidomics
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Dwarfing; programmed cell death; elevated defense signaling may penalize growth
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: FB1 treatment; Pieris brassicae egg extract; drought and cold stress in rice
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: LOH2 products are enriched in GlcCers and may also function as a safety valve to shunt LCBs into less bioactive forms
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong candidate for engineering inducible defense-associated sphingolipid flux, but should be controlled spatially or inducibly to avoid growth penalties

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: LAG1 homolog; Class II ceramide synthase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Class II ceramide synthesis; trihydroxy LCB-VLCFA ceramide branch
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Trihydroxy LCB-VLCFA ceramides / GIPC-enriched ceramide pool
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports basal membrane sphingolipid functions and contributes to homeostatic repression of SPT
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Midstream branch point toward Class II ceramides
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-defining enzyme, not explicitly rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: N-acylates trihydroxy long-chain bases with VLCFA-CoAs to form ceramides
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms branch ceramide products that repress upstream SPT via ORM
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports production of homeostatic phytoceramide-type ceramides without causing sphingolipid hyperaccumulation when overexpressed
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LOH3 is a Class II ceramide synthase with specificity similar to LOH1. 2. Along with LOH1, it generates trihydroxy LCB-VLCFA ceramides proposed to bind ORM and repress SPT. 3. Overexpression does not cause sphingolipid hyperaccumulation, unlike LOH2.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression analysis; substrate specificity studies; sphingolipidomics
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altering LOH3 may affect growth-related sphingolipid homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: LOH3 is less highly expressed than LOH1 in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential component for balancing defense and growth when stacked with other sphingolipid regulators

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Long-chain base modification; trihydroxy LCB formation
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream modification step before Class II ceramide synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates sphinganine at the C-4 position to form phytosphingosine
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for Class II ceramide synthase products that repress SPT
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss or suppression reduces trihydroxy LCB production, shifts flux to LOH2-derived dihydroxy ceramides, and causes sphingolipid hyperaccumulation and dwarfing
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SBH catalyzes formation of trihydroxy LCBs from sphinganine. 2. Disruption of SBH reduces trihydroxy LCB availability and causes hyperaccumulation of LOH2-type dihydroxy ceramides. 3. This supports the model that trihydroxy-LCB-derived ceramides are needed for ORM-mediated repression of SPT.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Dwarfing and defense activation due to sphingolipid imbalance
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Parallel evidence from Physcomitrella patens sbh mutant supports conserved role of trihydroxy LCBs in homeostatic regulation
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Engineering SBH can shift defense-versus-growth sphingolipid balance but may strongly affect development

Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: Negative regulator of serine palmitoyltransferase; ER membrane-associated sphingolipid sensor
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Orosomucoid-like protein with four transmembrane domains
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Membrane homeostasis; control of PCD and pathogen responses via LCB/ceramide balance
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Ceramide-dependent competitive inhibition of SPT activity
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses SPT enzymatic activity through interaction with LCB1/LCB2-containing complex
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Primary sensor and negative regulator controlling when de novo sphingolipid biosynthesis is repressed
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Restrains LCB and ceramide accumulation; loss causes uncontrolled LCB biosynthesis, ceramide hyperaccumulation, nonviable seeds, defense activation, and increased resistance to Pseudomonas syringae
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ORM1 is a core negative regulator of Arabidopsis SPT and senses intracellular ceramides through direct binding. 2. Cryo-EM showed ceramide-bound ORM1 adopts an inhibitory conformation that blocks palmitoyl-CoA entry into the SPT active site. 3. Loss or RNAi suppression of ORM function deregulates sphingolipid biosynthesis, elevates LCBs and LOH2-derived ceramides, and enhances defense-associated phenotypes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene-edited mutants; RNAi lines; cryo-EM structural analysis; in vitro SPT activity assays with defined ceramides; sphingolipid profiling
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: LCB1; LCB2a; LCB2b; ssSPTa; ORM2; FLS2; ERAD complex component(s)
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: SPT-ORM membrane complex dimer with LCB1, LCB2, ssSPTa, and ORM1
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Intracellular ceramide concentration and structure; environmental perturbations linked to pathogen attack
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Negative feedback homeostatic regulation via ceramide sensing
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Complete loss impairs seed viability and development; suppression can cause early senescence and growth defects
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: FB1 treatment; pathogen infection; insect egg extract-associated PCD contexts
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review also cites possible non-SPT roles in autophagy receptor function and ER-associated degradation regulation
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising leverage point for tuning defense-associated sphingolipid bursts, but manipulation must avoid lethal overaccumulation; inducible or partial modulation is preferable

Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: Negative regulator of serine palmitoyltransferase; ER membrane-associated sphingolipid sensor
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Orosomucoid-like protein with four transmembrane domains
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Membrane homeostasis; control of PCD and pathogen responses via LCB/ceramide balance
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses SPT enzymatic activity together with ORM1
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential homeostatic regulator whose combined loss with ORM1 causes catastrophic sphingolipid deregulation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Suppresses excess sphingolipid biosynthesis; combined loss with ORM1 causes nonviable seeds and free ceramide hyperaccumulation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ORM2 functions with ORM1 as an essential negative regulator of SPT. 2. Gene-edited loss of both ORM genes causes uncontrolled LCB biosynthesis and free ceramide hyperaccumulation. 3. Reduced ORM activity enhances defense-associated phenotypes and resistance to Pseudomonas syringae.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene-edited mutants; RNAi lines; sphingolipid profiling
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions within or alongside the SPT-ORM complex
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong loss impairs viability and development
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review treats ORM1/ORM2 together genetically, though structural details are primarily from ORM1 complex
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Partial repression or context-specific editing may enhance immunity, but severe deregulation is deleterious
